main_ingredients: tempura batter (wheat flour, cold water, egg)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of ebi tempura consists of 4 to 5 battered and deep-fried prawns weighing approximately 150 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Tempura, shrimp — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 35157)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for shrimp tempura (battered, deep-fried). Values are expressed per 100 g edible portion. This is the closest approved-source match for ebi tempura; no Singapore HPB Foodo entry for ebi tempura was found. Minor rounding applied for consistency.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for ebi tempura was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is estimated by analogy with battered and deep-fried seafood items and wheat-flour-based batter foods (GI range 40–55 in peer-reviewed literature). The batter contributes refined wheat flour carbohydrates; however, the high fat and protein content of the dish significantly attenuates the glycemic response. GI 45 (low category, ≤55) is a conservative and defensible estimate consistent with similar fried battered seafood entries in the Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~570 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit ebi tempura to occasional consumption, as a single serving contributes approximately 570 mg sodium — roughly 25% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — and portion control or low-sodium dipping sauce substitution is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour batter contributing moderate carbohydrate load (GI ~45, GL ~12.6 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume ebi tempura in moderated portions, as the wheat-flour batter provides a glycemic load of approximately 12.6 per standard serving; pairing with non-starchy vegetables and avoiding high-sugar dipping sauces can help manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying oil absorption elevating total fat (~11.8 g/100 g) and cholesterol (~115 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit ebi tempura intake due to its elevated total fat and cholesterol content from deep-frying; choosing air-fried or oven-baked alternatives and limiting frequency to once per week is recommended to support cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ebi Tempura (海老天ぷら) is a classic Japanese dish consisting of whole prawns (ebi) coated in a light, airy tempura batter made from cold water, wheat flour, and egg, then deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crisp. The dish is a staple of Japanese cuisine and is widely served in Japanese restaurants across Singapore and globally. The batter is intentionally thin and delicate, producing a characteristic lacy, crispy texture that contrasts with the tender, juicy prawn inside. Ebi tempura is commonly served as a standalone appetiser, as part of a tempura platter, over rice as a donburi (ebi tendon), or alongside soba or udon noodles. It is typically accompanied by a light tentsuyu dipping sauce made from dashi, mirin, and soy sauce, with grated daikon radish. Nutritionally, ebi tempura provides moderate protein (~12.5 g/100 g) from the prawn, moderate carbohydrates (~18.6 g/100 g) from the batter, and elevated fat (~11.8 g/100 g) from deep-frying. Sodium content is moderate (~380 mg/100 g) and increases further with dipping sauce. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low category), though the glycemic load per serving (~12.6) is clinically relevant for individuals managing blood glucose. Clinically, ebi tempura is a moderate-concern food for hypertension due to sodium, for Type 2 Diabetes due to refined carbohydrate batter, and for cardiovascular disease due to fat and cholesterol from frying. It is a good source of selenium, phosphorus, and vitamin B12 from the prawn component. The dish is not suitable for individuals with crustacean, gluten, or egg allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in ebi tempura?
Is ebi tempura high in sodium?
What is the GI of ebi tempura?
What allergens are in ebi tempura?
What is the difference between ebi tempura and prawn tempura?

confidence_reason: No exact ebi tempura entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for shrimp tempura (battered, deep-fried), which is the closest approved-source match. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for shrimp tempura. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with numeric estimates from the USDA source. The input food_name '751D Ebi Tempura(D)' appears to be a menu code from GyoGyo - Funan restaurant on field survey data; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Ebi Tempura'. The '751D' prefix and '(D)' suffix are likely internal restaurant menu identifiers and have been preserved in aliases. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for ebi tempura exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 27.9) / 100 ≈ 12.6, where carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 18.6 × 1.5 = 27.9 g.
